This chapter concerns itself entirely with the kingdom of Israel and its changes. God still calls Israel his people, even though they are terribly corrupted. Jehu predicts the same destruction will come upon Baasha's family, which that king had been employed to bring upon Jeroboam’s family. Those who resemble others in their sins may expect to resemble them in the troubles they suffer, especially those who seem zealous against such sins in others while allowing them in themselves. Baasha himself dies peacefully and is buried with honor. This clearly shows that there are punishments after death, which should be feared most. Let Elah serve as a warning to drunkards, who do not know that death may surprise them. Death easily comes upon people when they are drunk. Besides the illnesses people bring on themselves by drinking, they are easily overcome by an enemy and liable to bad accidents while in that state. Death comes terribly upon people in such a state, finding them in the act of sin and unfit for any act of devotion; that day comes upon them unexpectedly. The word of God was fulfilled, and the sins of Baasha and Elah were accounted for, with which they provoked God. Their idols are called their vanities, because idols cannot profit or help; miserable are those whose gods are vanities.

When people forsake God, they will be left to plague one another. Proud, ambitious people ruin one another. Omri struggled with Tibni for some years. Though we do not always understand the rules by which God governs nations and individuals in his providence, we may learn useful lessons from the history before us. When tyrants succeed each other, and massacres, conspiracies, and civil wars occur, we can be sure that the Lord has a controversy with the people for their sins; they are urgently called to repent and reform. Omri made himself infamous because of his wickedness. Many wicked people have been men of power and renown; they have built cities, and their names are found in history; but they have no name in the book of life.

Ahab did evil more than anyone who reigned before him, and he did it with a particular hatred for both Jehovah and Israel. He was not satisfied with breaking the second commandment by worshipping idols; he broke the first by worshipping other gods. Taking light of smaller sins leads to greater ones. Marriages with bold offenders also encourage wickedness and hurry people on to the greatest excesses. One of Ahab’s subjects, following his example of presumption, dared to build Jericho. Like Achan, he meddled with what was forbidden; he used for his own purposes that which was devoted to God's honor. He began to build in defiance of the curse well known in Israel; but no one ever hardened his heart against God and prospered. Let reading this chapter cause us to notice the dreadful end of all who work iniquity. And what does the history of all ungodly people furnish, whatever rank or position they hold, but sad examples of the same?
